title: The Silicon Silk Road
narrative: The invention of the transistor in Murray Hill, USA, ignited a chain reaction that crossed the Pacific. American semiconductor technology was exported to the manufacturing powerhouses of East Asia — South Korea and Taiwan — who transformed it into the engines of the 'Asian Tiger' economies. This invisible arc of knowledge defined what we now call the Digital Age.

title: The Democratic Wave
narrative: The fall of the Berlin Wall was not just an event — it was a signal heard across Eurasia. Within weeks, the Velvet Revolution peacefully swept Czechoslovakia. Within two years, the Soviet Union itself dissolved in Moscow. This arc represents the fastest and most dramatic domino effect of democratic contagion in recorded history.

origin.label: Berlin, Germany (Fall of the Berlin Wall)

targets.label: Moscow, Russia (Dissolution of the Soviet Union, 1991)

title: The Maritime Pulse
narrative: At the Naval School of Sagres, Portuguese navigators spent decades perfecting cartography, astronomy, and ship design. This concentrated scientific effort produced results visible across the globe: Vasco da Gama's landing at Calicut, India in 1498 opened permanent sea routes to Asia, while Cabral's arrival in Brazil in 1500 planted Europe's foot in the Americas. One school; two continents changed.

title: The Enlightenment Circuit
narrative: When Diderot and d'Alembert published the Encyclopédie in Paris in 1751, they codified the Enlightenment's core ideas: Reason, Liberty, and Natural Rights. These ideas crossed the Atlantic carried by philosophers, diplomats, and soldiers. Just 25 years later, Jefferson transmuted them into the American Declaration of Independence in Philadelphia — an intellectual arc that rewired the concept of governance forever.

title: The Green Revolution
narrative: Norman Borlaug's research station in Texcoco, Mexico developed high-yield, disease-resistant wheat varieties that would change the world. When this technology was transferred to the farms of Punjab, India in the 1960s, food production doubled within a decade. This arc of agricultural science prevented mass famine for hundreds of millions of people across South Asia, fundamentally altering global demographics.
